`Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent` is evaluated twice — once for `_pkg_templates` and once for `_repo_templates`. Extracting it to a named variable makes the relationship between the two paths explicit and avoids repeating the same expression.

The expression `(text[-tail:] if tail else "")` requires a conditional guard because `text[-0:]` returns the full string rather than an empty string. Since `text_len` is already computed, `text[text_len - tail:]` naturally handles both cases: when tail>0 it returns the last `tail` chars, and when tail==0 it returns `text[text_len:]` which is `""`. This removes the conditional entirely.

In `kotib_transcribe`, `import re` is executed inside the function body, but `re` is already imported at the module level (line 13). The redundant local import is dead weight — removing it makes the function use the already-available module-level import.

The `if __name__ == '__main__':` guard in `__main__.py` is always true — Python only executes `__main__.py` when the package is run directly (`python -m qanot`), so the module name is always `'__main__'`. The guard adds noise without providing any protection.

_VERBOSE_KEYS is a constant frozenset defined inside _strip_verbose_result on every call. Moving it to module level makes its immutability explicit and avoids recreating the set object on each invocation.

In `backup_workspace`, `ws / f` and `ws / d` are each evaluated twice per iteration — once in the filter condition and once in the list element. Using the walrus operator eliminates the redundant Path construction.

In `_config_set`, `value.lower()` is called twice — once for the 'true' check and once for the 'false' check. Extracting it to a variable avoids the redundant method call and makes the intent clearer.

Inside `load_config`, `import re` and a local `_CONTROL_CHAR_RE` are defined, but both already exist at module level (lines 6 and 11). The local definitions shadow the module-level constant and redundantly recompile the regex on every call to `load_config`.

In CostTracker, `import json` is duplicated inside both `_load` and `_save`. Moving it to the module-level imports (alongside the other stdlib imports) eliminates the repeated local imports and follows standard Python convention.

In _generate_systemd and _generate_launchd, `config_path.resolve()` is called twice — once for `config` and once for `working_dir`. Extracting it to a local variable avoids the redundant resolve() call.

`import asyncio` and `import json` are both unused in this file. Async function syntax does not require an explicit asyncio import, and all JSON responses are produced via aiohttp's `web.json_response` which handles serialization internally. Removing them eliminates dead imports.

In the Config section, `ci.innerHTML +=` inside a forEach loop causes repeated DOM reflows and reparse on every iteration. The Tools and Memory sections already use `.map().join('')` for a single assignment — the Config section should match that pattern for consistency and to avoid O(n) DOM mutations.

In `safe_write_file`, `resolved` is computed with an unnecessary conditional: `os.path.realpath(path) if os.path.exists(os.path.dirname(path)) else path`. `os.path.realpath()` does not require the path to exist — it resolves symlinks for whatever portions of the path do exist and returns a normalized absolute path for the rest. The guard adds complexity and diverges from the unconditional call in `validate_write_path`. Removing it simplifies the line and makes the two functions consistent.

With `errors='replace'`, Python never raises UnicodeDecodeError — it silently substitutes replacement characters instead. Only LookupError (unknown codec name) can escape. The UnicodeDecodeError in the except tuple is dead code and misleads readers into thinking the fallback handles encoding failures.
The intermediate `names` variable is assigned only to be used in the very next line's `join()`. Inlining the generator eliminates the one-use variable and reduces visual noise.

In `write_daily_note`, the uid_tag is computed inline with a duplicated expression that already exists in the `_uid_tag` helper. Using the helper eliminates the DRY violation and keeps the logic in one place.
`import functools` is present at the top of the file but is never used anywhere — neither in the `tool` decorator, `_collect_tools`, nor any other function. Removing it eliminates a dead import.

In `_deploy_plugin_files`, `open(soul_path, ...)` is used to append to SOUL.md, but `soul_path` is already a `Path` object. The same function already uses the Path API everywhere else (`soul_path.exists()`, `soul_path.read_text()`). Using `soul_path.open(...)` instead of the built-in `open(soul_path, ...)` is consistent with the surrounding code.
In `chat`, the Usage object is constructed manually inline, duplicating the logic already encapsulated in `_build_usage`. Using the helper eliminates the DRY violation.

In `Usage.__post_init__`, the same `max(0, int(...))` pattern is repeated four times for the token fields. A loop over the field names eliminates the repetition and makes adding new token fields easier.

The `or` operator for extracting the HTTP status attribute treats any falsy value (e.g. 0) on `status_code` as absent and falls through to `status`. Using an explicit `is None` guard makes the intent clear and avoids the subtle falsy-value edge case.

The 4-line block that resolves available providers and builds the try order is duplicated identically in both `chat` and `chat_stream`. Extracting it into a `_resolve_try_order` helper eliminates the repetition and keeps the fallback logic in one place.

`_sanitize_gemini_tools` is a one-liner used exactly once, in `_prepare_tools`. The wrapper adds indirection without clarity — the method's docstring already explains the operation. Inlining it removes a needless intermediate name.

In __init__, `.strip()` is called twice for both `api_key` and `model`: once inside the validation condition and again for the assignment. Using the walrus operator strips-and-assigns in a single step, eliminating the redundant calls and the separate assignment lines.

`text_parts_str` is assigned only to be immediately passed to `join()`. Inlining the generator removes the one-use variable and reduces visual noise, consistent with the same pattern already applied in main.py.

The `__all__` list uses case-insensitive alphabetical ordering, which interleaves function names (snake_case) with class names (PascalCase). Python convention is case-sensitive ASCII ordering, which naturally groups all PascalCase class names before snake_case function names, making the type of each export visually clear at a glance.

In `BM25Index.search`, `query_tokens` may contain duplicate terms (e.g. 'the cat and the dog'). The inner loop adds each token's BM25 contribution for every occurrence, artificially inflating scores. Standard BM25 scores unique query terms only — iterating over `set(query_tokens)` fixes the double-counting bug.

The batch size of 100 is a magic number duplicated four times across GeminiEmbedder.embed and OpenAIEmbedder.embed (range step and slice end). Extracting it as a module-level constant makes the shared limit explicit and ensures a single change point if it ever needs tuning.

In `_embed_with_cache`, the result reassembly loop uses a manual `miss_idx` counter to step through `new_embeddings`. An iterator over `new_embeddings` replaces the mutable counter and compresses the loop into a readable list comprehension.

In `_index_file`, the dict `_indexed_hashes` is looked up twice for the same key: once with `.get()` for the hash comparison and again with `in` to decide whether to delete stale chunks. Capturing the result of `.get()` in a local variable `old_hash` removes the duplicate lookup and lets the second condition use `old_hash is not None`, which is both semantically clearer and DRYer.

In `delete_source`, the FTS5 deletion loop iterates over every chunk ID fetched from `chunks` and issues a separate DELETE per row. Since `chunks_fts` already stores `source` as an UNINDEXED column, a single `DELETE FROM chunks_fts WHERE source = ?` replaces the entire loop. The per-rowid vec loop is still needed (sqlite-vec is rowid-keyed), but we only need to fetch rowids when vec is available.

In `check`, `self._requests.get(user_id, [])` followed by `self._requests[user_id] = timestamps` can be simplified using `setdefault` to get-or-create in one step, then mutate in place via list slice assignment, removing the redundant dict write.

In `classify_complexity`, `len([s for s in re.split(...) if s.strip()])` builds a temporary list just to measure its length. Using `sum(1 for ...)` avoids the allocation and is idiomatic Python for counting filtered items.

In `_run_isolated`, the local variable `content` is reused for two unrelated reads: first for HEARTBEAT.md content (in the early-exit guard), then for outbox content lower in the function. Renaming the outbox read to `outbox_content` eliminates the shadowing, making each variable's purpose immediately clear.

When `value` is a dict with neither 'env' nor 'file' key, the function silently falls through to `str(value)`, returning a stringified dict like "{'bad_key': 'x'}" instead of failing fast. An unrecognised secret format is a misconfiguration and should raise a clear ValueError rather than silently produce a garbage string.

In `new_session`, the date format string `"%Y-%m-%d"` and the `datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime(...)` call duplicate the same logic already in the `session_id` property. Since the property auto-generates the date-based ID when `_session_id is None`, setting `self._session_id = session_id` (which may be None) lets the property handle default generation, eliminating the duplicate.

In the batch coalescing block, `texts` is a temporary list built solely to be passed to `str.join`. Since `join` accepts any iterable, a generator expression eliminates the intermediate list allocation.

In `send_file`, the error message hardcodes '50MB' as a magic string that duplicates the value already expressed in `MAX_FILE_SIZE`. Additionally, `size / 1024 / 1024` is computed twice. Computing the MB value once and deriving the max from the constant removes both the duplication and the risk of the message becoming stale if the constant changes.

cron_delete and cron_update call scheduler_ref.reload_jobs() directly, duplicating the null-check and hasattr guard already encapsulated in _reload_scheduler(). Using the helper removes the duplication and keeps the reload logic in one place.

In `_mirror_to_group`, the `elif direction == 'result'`, `elif direction == 'turn'`, and `else` branches all produce identical code `msg = text[:3500]`. Collapsing them into a single `else` removes three-way duplication and makes clear that only 'delegate' and 'converse' get special prefixes.

In `_check_rag`, `details.append(f"enabled: {config.rag_enabled}")` is dead code on the disabled path (the early return constructs a completely separate dict that ignores `details`) and redundant noise on the enabled path (the subsequent db checks already imply RAG is active). Removing it eliminates the misleading append-before-discard pattern.

In `_find_last_image_in_conversation`, `source.get("data")` is called to check truthiness in the `if` condition, then `source["data"]` performs a redundant second dict lookup inside `base64.b64decode`. Storing the result in a local variable `data` eliminates the double lookup and makes the decode call self-evident.

`json.dumps` followed by `write_text` builds the entire JSON string in memory before writing. Using `json.dump` directly to a file object is more idiomatic and avoids the intermediate string allocation.

In `rag_search`, the `top_k` validation conflates a type guard with a range check in a single `or` condition, obscuring the two separate concerns. Splitting them — type check sets the default, valid ints get clamped with `max`/`min` — makes each branch's intent immediately clear and treats out-of-range ints consistently (clamped to boundary rather than silently replaced with the default).

MAX_TASK_CHARS is defined as a local constant inside `spawn_sub_agent`, inconsistent with the other module-level limit constants (MAX_CONCURRENT_PER_USER, SUB_AGENT_TIMEOUT, MAX_RESULT_CHARS). Moving it to module level makes all limits visible in one place and follows the existing pattern.

The expression `FETCH_MAX_BODY // (1024 * 1024)` is computed twice inline inside `web_fetch` for the error messages. Extracting it as a module-level constant `FETCH_MAX_BODY_MB` eliminates the duplication and keeps the error messages consistent with the constant automatically if `FETCH_MAX_BODY` ever changes.

In update_session_state, the non-update branch opens a separate append-mode file handle even though `content` was already read into memory. Using write_text with `content + new_line` in the else branch makes both branches use the same write path and eliminates the inconsistent open() call.

The tail slice `text[text_len - tail:]` is equivalent to `text[-tail:]` when tail > 0, but requires the redundant `text_len` variable. Using `text[-tail:]` is more idiomatic Python. However, when tail is 0, `text[-0:]` returns the full string, so the guard `if tail else ''` is needed. Alternatively, keep text_len but drop the redundant variable by inlining it. A cleaner fix: replace `text[text_len - tail:]` with `text[-tail:]` and handle the tail==0 case explicitly, making the slice intent clearer.

In `aisha_tts`, `json` is imported locally as `_json` inside the function body, but `json` is already imported at the module level. The redundant local import can be removed and replaced with the already-available module-level `json`.

There are two definitions of `_strip_verbose_result`: an empty stub (docstring-only body) followed by `_VERBOSE_KEYS`, then the real implementation. The first definition is dead code — Python uses the second definition, silently shadowing the first. Removing the stub eliminates the confusing duplicate.

In `_typing_loop`, the inner `except asyncio.CancelledError: raise` is redundant in Python 3.8+ because `asyncio.CancelledError` inherits from `BaseException`, not `Exception`, so the surrounding `except Exception` block would never catch it anyway. Removing the redundant guard simplifies the try/except structure.

In `_rotate_backups`, `shutil.rmtree(str(oldest))` converts a Path to a string unnecessarily. `shutil.rmtree` accepts Path objects directly since Python 3.6, and the rest of the file passes Path objects to shutil functions without conversion.

In `_config_set`, `value.isdigit()` only returns True for non-negative digit strings (e.g. it fails for "-1"), so negative integers are incorrectly left as strings. Replacing it with a `try: int(value)` block handles the full integer range and simplifies the branching by removing the separate `isdigit` guard.

The three numeric-field validation blocks repeat the same isinstance+range check pattern with different variable names. Extracting them into a loop over a tuple of (field, lo, hi) eliminates the duplication and makes it trivial to add future bounds checks.

In CostTracker._load and _save, `import json` is repeated as a local import inside each method, but `json` is already imported at the module level (line 6). These redundant local imports can be removed.

In daemon_start, daemon_stop, and daemon_status, the Windows task name is computed inline as `f"Qanot_{name}"` instead of using the existing `_task_name()` helper. This duplicates the naming logic and diverges from daemon_install/daemon_uninstall which correctly use `_task_name(name)`.

The costs user list uses `innerHTML +=` inside a forEach loop, causing repeated DOM re-renders. All other dynamic sections in load() already use map/join with a single innerHTML assignment. Replacing forEach+innerHTML+= with map+join makes this consistent and avoids the anti-pattern.

In validate_write_path, `parent = os.path.dirname(resolved)` where `resolved = os.path.realpath(path)`. Since realpath resolves ALL symlinks in the path, its dirname can never itself be a symlink, making `os.path.islink(parent)` always False. The `(os.path.exists(parent) and os.path.islink(parent))` branch is dead code. Removing it and the unused `parent` variable simplifies the symlink guard to just the meaningful check on the original `path`.

After truncating a preview to fit within max_total_chars, the reassignment of `preview_len` on line 294 is dead computation — the truncated preview is the last one that can fit, so we should `break` immediately after appending it instead of recomputing preview_len and continuing the loop (which would just break on the next iteration anyway due to exhausted budget).

The lambda `lambda: agent.current_user_id` is repeated identically in three consecutive tool-registration calls. Extracting it into a named variable eliminates the duplication and makes it trivially easy to change the accessor in one place.

In write_daily_note, datetime.now(timezone.utc) is called twice — once for 'today' (the date) and once for 'ts' (the time). A single call captures both values atomically and avoids a redundant syscall, while also preventing an (unlikely but possible) date mismatch if the call straddles midnight.

In the `tool` decorator, `parameters or default` replaces an empty dict `{}` with the default because empty dicts are falsy. An explicit `is not None` check correctly distinguishes 'not provided' from 'provided but empty'.
In `_check_required_config`, `not config.get(key)` treats falsy-but-present values (0, False, "") as missing keys. The intent is to check key presence, so `key not in config` is the correct guard.

experiments.hypothesis: _truncate_content is a single-line pass-through wrapper around truncate_with_marker with no added logic. It adds an indirection layer without value — _add can call truncate_with_marker directly.

In chat_stream, the final ProviderResponse constructs Usage inline instead of calling the existing _build_usage() helper, duplicating the field-mapping logic that chat() already delegates to _build_usage().

In Usage.__post_init__, the loop with getattr/setattr uses dynamic attribute access for static, known field names. Direct attribute assignment is more explicit, avoids runtime string-to-attribute lookup overhead, and makes the normalization logic immediately readable without needing to trace the loop.

The two isinstance checks for built-in exception types both map single types to single error codes. They can be unified into a single dict-based dispatch that eliminates the repeated if/return pattern and makes adding new built-in exception mappings a one-line data change instead of a new if-branch.

chat_stream duplicates the _resolve_try_order() logic inline — it manually calls _get_available_indices(), handles the empty-available fallback, and calls _build_try_order(), which is exactly what _resolve_try_order() already encapsulates. Replacing the three lines with a single _resolve_try_order() call removes the duplication.

_prepare_tools calls _sanitize_gemini_tools which is not defined anywhere in this file and not imported — it would raise a NameError at runtime. The function should compose the two already-available helpers: _anthropic_tools_to_openai (imported) to convert the tool schema format, then _strip_unsupported_keys (defined above) to remove Gemini-incompatible JSON Schema keys.

The `_calc_cost` parameter names `inp` and `out` are abbreviated to avoid shadowing the builtin `input`, but `input_tokens` and `output_tokens` are self-documenting and eliminate the need to mentally decode abbreviations when reading the cost formula.

The `_calc_cost` method uses abbreviated parameter names `inp` and `out` that require mental decoding. Using `input_tokens` and `output_tokens` makes the cost formula self-documenting, consistent with how the method is called (where the variables are also named `inp`/`out` but represent token counts).

In __all__, chunk_text and create_embedder are placed at the bottom after all class names, breaking case-insensitive alphabetical order. Sorting case-insensitively groups entries by their natural position (chunk_text near BM25Index, create_embedder near Embedder) making the list easier to scan.

In BM25Index.search, `set(query_tokens)` is recomputed inside the per-document loop, so it gets reconstructed N times (once per document). Moving it outside the loop computes it once and makes the intent clearer.

_EMBED_BATCH_SIZE = 100 is defined as a module-level constant but never used — both GeminiEmbedder.embed() and OpenAIEmbedder.embed() hardcode 100 directly. Using the constant makes the batch size a single source of truth and makes the constant's purpose evident.

In _text_similarity, the union set is materialised only to call len() on it. Using the identity |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B| avoids allocating the union set entirely and makes the Jaccard formula self-evident.

In _index_file, the comment 'Delete stale chunks only when a prior version exists, then ingest fresh content' restates exactly what the surrounding if-block and ingest call already show. Removing it reduces noise without losing any information.

In delete_source, fetching all rowids and deleting them one-by-one in a Python loop is unnecessary. A single DELETE with a subquery eliminates the fetch, the loop variable, and the repeated round-trips to SQLite.

In cleanup(), the loop variable `t` in the _locked_until comprehension is ambiguous — the comment on line 37 names these values `unlock_time`, and check() also uses that name. Using `unlock_time` throughout makes the comprehension consistent with the rest of the class.

`effective = max(msg_score, ctx_score)` is computed on line 175 but never used — all subsequent branches reference `msg_score` and `ctx_score` directly. Removing it eliminates dead code and avoids implying the combined score drives routing.

The magic number 300 in _is_heartbeat_ok is unexplained inline. Extracting it as a named module-level constant documents its purpose (max response length that still counts as 'nothing to report') and makes it easy to find if the threshold ever needs tuning.

The `secret_fields` set in `resolve_config_secrets` is a plain local set literal with no signal that it's a configuration point. Promoting it to a module-level `frozenset` constant `_SECRET_FIELDS` makes it immutable (signalling it shouldn't be mutated), easier to find when adding new secret fields, and consistent with the module-level `logger` pattern already in the file.

The conversation-key formula `f'group_{chat_id}' if is_group else str(user_id)` is duplicated in both _handle_message and _check_command_access. Extracting it to a private _conv_key() helper gives the concept a name, eliminates the duplication, and ensures both callers always stay in sync.

_TRUTHY_FIELDS and _DEFAULT_FIELDS are named as constants (ALL_CAPS) but defined as local variables inside _save_agents_to_config, where they are recreated on every call. Promoting them to true module-level constants avoids repeated allocation and puts them next to other module-level definitions where they belong.

_is_dangerous_command and _needs_approval are structurally identical — both iterate a list of (pattern, description) pairs and return the first matching description. Extracting the shared loop into a private _first_match helper eliminates the duplication and makes each public function a one-liner that names which list it delegates to.

cron_update calls scheduler_ref.reload_jobs() inline, duplicating the logic already encapsulated in the _reload_scheduler() helper. cron_create and cron_delete both use that helper; cron_update should too for consistency and DRY.

In `converse_with_agent`, `depth = current_depth + 1` is computed at the top of the function but the `_log_activity` call immediately below uses `current_depth + 1` again instead of the already-computed `depth` variable. This duplicates the arithmetic and is inconsistent with `delegate_to_agent`, which correctly uses `depth` in its own `_log_activity` call.

Three f-strings in _check_config have no interpolation placeholders — the f-prefix is unnecessary and misleads readers into looking for substitutions that don't exist. Dropping the prefix makes the intent clear.

In _find_last_image_in_conversation, `msg.get("content", "")` uses an empty string as the default, but the only thing the next line checks is whether content is a list. The default value `""` implies the string has meaning, when in fact any non-list value leads to `continue`. Using `None` as the default is more accurate and avoids implying string semantics.

The temp path is constructed via string concatenation on the filename (`jobs_path.with_name(jobs_path.name + ".tmp")`). Pathlib's `with_suffix` can express the same intent more idiomatically: append `.tmp` as an additional suffix rather than manually concatenating to the raw name string.

In rag_search, `user_id or None` normalises an empty string to None before passing to engine.query. In rag_index the same get_user_id() result is passed to engine.ingest without that guard, creating inconsistent behaviour: an empty-string user_id would be stored during indexing but treated as None during search. Applying the same normalisation in rag_index makes both callers consistent.

MAX_TASK_CHARS is defined at module level (line 25) and then re-defined as a local variable inside spawn_sub_agent, shadowing the module-level constant. The inner redefinition is redundant and masks the fact that there is already a single source of truth for this limit.

FETCH_MAX_BODY_MB is defined as a module-level constant for exactly this purpose, but both 'Response too large' error messages inline the same arithmetic (FETCH_MAX_BODY // (1024 * 1024)) instead of using it. Replacing the inline divisions with the existing constant removes the duplication and keeps the module-level definition as the single source of truth.
The two branches of the if/else both call state_path.write_text(..., encoding='utf-8'), duplicating the write call. Computing new_content first and writing once removes the repetition.
tail_text is a one-use variable assigned immediately before the return statement. Inlining it removes a redundant name without affecting readability, since the expression is already self-explanatory.

_ALLOWED_HOSTS is defined as a local variable inside download_audio on every call, but it is a fixed set that never changes. Moving it to module level as a constant makes it a single source of truth, avoids re-creating the set on each invocation, and follows the same pattern used for MUXLISA_BASE_URL, KOTIB_BASE_URL, etc.

The `main()` call in __main__.py is not guarded by `if __name__ == '__main__':`. While __main__.py is always executed as the main module via `python -m qanot`, the guard is the standard Python convention for entry-point files and prevents accidental execution if the file is ever imported directly.

reset() with a specific user_id duplicates the three-line body of _remove_user_state(), which exists precisely to encapsulate that operation. Delegating to _remove_user_state() removes the duplication and keeps the two code paths in sync automatically.

user_id is a one-use variable in _handle_reset, assigned immediately before the single call that consumes it. Inlining it removes the redundant name without affecting readability, consistent with the same pattern already applied in utils.py.

`p` in `get_voice_api_key` is a one-use variable assigned immediately before the return statement. Inlining it removes the redundant name, consistent with the same simplification already applied elsewhere in the project (utils.py, agent_bot.py, backup.py, cli.py).

In needs_compaction, `estimated_next` is a one-use variable assigned immediately before the return statement. Inlining it removes the redundant name, consistent with the same simplification already applied elsewhere in the project (utils.py, agent_bot.py, backup.py, cli.py, config.py).

In is_path_within_root, `real_root` and `real_path` are one-use variables assigned immediately before the return statement. In extract_urls, `after_scheme.split("/", 1)[0]` is computed twice: once for the credentials check and once for host_part. Extracting it as `netloc` (the standard urllib.parse name for host:port) eliminates the duplicate split and makes both uses clearer.

get_user_id lambda is defined as a standalone variable but is identical to `lambda: agent.current_user_id` already used inline in register_rag_tools and register_image_tools. However, the real duplication is that `get_user_id` is defined after _telegram_ref.append(telegram) but the same lambda expression `lambda: agent.current_user_id` already appears twice above it. The is_dup check in _append_to_memory uses an if/else branch to handle short vs long detail strings, but the two cases can be unified into a single any() expression, removing the branch without changing behavior.
_collect_tools uses a loop+append pattern that can be replaced with a list comprehension, which is more idiomatic Python and removes the intermediate accumulator variable.

The for-loop-with-break that searches for a Plugin subclass is a `next()` with generator pattern. Replacing it with `next()` removes the intermediate `attr` binding and the manual `break`, making it more idiomatic Python — consistent with the _collect_tools improvement in plugins/base.py.

`session_info` is built in two steps: an initial assignment then a conditional `+=` mutation before being appended. Eliminating the intermediate variable and the mutation by embedding the conditional warning directly in `parts.append()` is more functional and consistent with the project's pattern of removing one-use variables.

In `chat`, `usage_dict` and `usage` are both one-use variables assigned immediately before the return statement. Inlining them removes the redundant names, consistent with the same simplification already applied elsewhere in the project.

In ToolCall.__post_init__, `val` is a one-use intermediate assigned solely to be consumed in the same expression. In `_calc_cost`, the fallback references `_GROQ_DEFAULT_PRICING` which does not exist — the variable defined at module level is `_DEFAULT_PRICING`. This causes a NameError at runtime for any unrecognised Groq model. Correcting the name to `_DEFAULT_PRICING` fixes the bug.

In `_calc_cost`, `/ 1_000_000` is repeated twice — once per token category. Factoring the common divisor outside the sum eliminates the repetition and mirrors the fix already applied to the Anthropic provider.

The temporal decay loop mutates `fused_scores[chunk_id]` while iterating over `fused_scores.items()`. Mutating a dict's values during iteration is technically safe in CPython only because the size doesn't change, but it is undefined behaviour per the language spec and flagged by linters. Wrapping the iterable in `list()` takes a snapshot before the loop begins, making the mutation explicit and safe across all Python implementations.

In `index_workspace`, `memory_dir = self.workspace_dir / "memory"` is a one-use intermediate assigned solely to be checked on the next line and reused in the same branch. A walrus operator captures it in the guard and reuses it in the loop body, removing the separate assignment — consistent with the project-wide pattern of using walrus for single-lookup guard checks.

In `cleanup`, the loop variable `ts` is a shortened alias for what everywhere else in the class is called `timestamps`. Using the full name in the dict comprehension makes the code consistent and self-documenting without changing behaviour.

In `_assess_context`, the `> 500` threshold check and `score += 0.2` increment are duplicated verbatim for the `str` and `list` content branches. Computing `text_len` uniformly first and then applying a single conditional eliminates the repetition.

The load-ensure-add loop is duplicated verbatim in both `start` and `reload_jobs`. Extracting it into a private `_load_and_add_jobs` helper eliminates the repetition and gives both call-sites a single point of change.

In `resolve_config_secrets`, `voice_keys[name]` is mutated while iterating over `voice_keys.items()`. Mutating dict values during iteration is technically safe in CPython (size doesn't change) but is undefined behaviour per the language spec and flagged by linters. Wrapping the iterable in `list()` takes a snapshot before the loop begins, making the mutation safe across all Python implementations — consistent with the same fix already applied in qanot/rag/engine.py.

The expression for extracting text blocks from a structured content list is duplicated verbatim in both the user and assistant branches of `_entries_to_messages`. Extracting it into a private `_extract_text_blocks` helper eliminates the duplication and gives both call-sites a single point of change.

In `_handle_message`, `coalesce_key` is computed manually as `f"group_{message.chat.id}" if is_group else str(user_id)`, which duplicates the logic already encapsulated in `_conv_key`. Using `self._conv_key(message)` eliminates the duplication and keeps the key derivation in one place.

In `_hot_launch_agent_bot`, the inline block that checks for and stops an existing bot duplicates the logic already encapsulated in `_stop_agent_bot`. Replacing the inline block with a call to `_stop_agent_bot` eliminates the duplication and keeps the stop logic in one place.

In `list_files`, `size` is gated by `if kind == "file"`, re-deriving a boolean from the string that was itself derived from `item.is_dir()`. Using `not item.is_dir()` directly removes the string-to-boolean indirection.

In `cron_delete`, the existence check `len(new_jobs) == len(jobs)` derives a boolean by comparing list lengths — an indirect way to ask 'was any job removed?'. Checking existence directly with `any()` before filtering expresses the intent clearly and avoids building the filtered list only to discard it on the error path.

In `view_project_board`, the loop with a negative guard (`if agent_filter and entry['agent_id'] != agent_filter: continue`) is a roundabout way to express a filter. A list comprehension with a positive condition (`if not agent_filter or entry['agent_id'] == agent_filter`) expresses the intent directly and removes the intermediate mutation of `entries`.

In `_check_rag`, the inline ternary inside the f-string (`{'available' if cursor.fetchone() is not None else 'not available'}`) is hard to read at a glance. Assigning `cursor.fetchone()` to a named variable `fts5_row` makes both the intent and the truthiness check clear without changing behaviour (`fetchone()` returns `None` or a tuple, so truthiness is equivalent to `is not None`).

In `_extract_image_from_response`, `elif part.text` skips the text of any part that also carries `inline_data`. Gemini can return a part that has both fields populated; using a second independent `if` ensures `response_text` is captured regardless of whether `inline_data` is present in the same part.

In `save_jobs`, the tmp path is built with string concatenation `jobs_path.with_name(jobs_path.name + ".tmp")`. Using `jobs_path.with_suffix(jobs_path.suffix + ".tmp")` is more idiomatic pathlib — it expresses the intent as a suffix manipulation rather than a full-name rebuild, and avoids repeating `jobs_path.name`.

In `rag_search`, the `top_k` validation uses a two-branch if/else that assigns `top_k` in both branches. Collapsing it into a single ternary expression expresses the intent — 'clamp if valid int, else default' — as one readable line without the asymmetric structure.

In `_run_sub_agent`, `task_preview` is built as `task[:200] + ("..." if len(task) > 200 else "")`, which unconditionally slices to 200 chars and then conditionally appends an empty string. The intent — 'use the full string unless it's too long' — is more clearly expressed by inverting the condition: return `task` unchanged when it fits, and only slice-and-append when it doesn't.

In `web_fetch`, the `aiohttp.ClientError` handler checks `isinstance(e, asyncio.TimeoutError)`, but `asyncio.TimeoutError` is not a subclass of `aiohttp.ClientError`, so this branch can never be reached — the separate `except asyncio.TimeoutError` clause below handles it. The dead isinstance check should be removed, leaving only the string-based check for aiohttp timeout variants (e.g. `aiohttp.ServerTimeoutError`).

The final `new_content` assignment is a long ternary that mixes two unrelated code paths on one line, making it hard to follow at a glance. Splitting into a plain if/else makes each branch immediately clear.

The inline ternary `(text[-tail:] if tail else "")` at the end of the return statement obscures intent — `text[-0:]` returns the full string, so the guard is load-bearing but easy to miss when scanning. Extracting it to a named variable makes the return statement straightforward and the edge-case handling explicit.

In `muxlisa_tts`, the speaker resolution first does a dict lookup (always returning 0 for digit strings since they're not in MUXLISA_VOICES), then unconditionally overwrites with `int(voice)` if the voice is a digit. The two branches are independent and should be expressed as an if/else: check `isdigit()` first to short-circuit the dict lookup entirely when a numeric voice ID is provided.

In `_handle_end_turn`, the ternary `final_text[:200] + "..." if len(final_text) > 200 else final_text` unconditionally slices to 200 chars and conditionally appends — matching the anti-pattern fixed in subagent.py. Inverting the condition (use full string when it fits, only truncate when too long) makes the intent immediately clear.

In `_send_response`, the guard `if not text or not text.strip()` is redundant: `"".strip()` returns `""` which is already falsy, so the `not text` short-circuit check adds nothing when `text` is typed as `str`. Simplifying to `if not text.strip()` makes the intent (skip blank/whitespace-only responses) immediately clear in a single expression.

In `_rotate_backups`, the guard `if len(backup_dirs) > MAX_BACKUPS:` is redundant. When `len(backup_dirs) <= MAX_BACKUPS`, the slice `backup_dirs[:-MAX_BACKUPS]` evaluates to an empty list (e.g. `[1,2,3,4,5][:-5] == []`), so the for loop simply does nothing. Removing the guard eliminates a redundant condition check and lets the slice express the intent directly.

In `_plugin_list`, discovered plugins are collected via a manual loop with `set.add()`. A set comprehension expresses the same intent more directly and eliminates the intermediate empty-set initialization.

In `summarize_in_stages`, the fallback excerpt `text[:2000] + '... [summarization failed, raw excerpt]'` unconditionally truncates and appends '...' even when `text` is shorter than 2000 chars. Inverting the condition — keep the full text when it fits, only slice-and-append when it doesn't — matches the pattern applied elsewhere in the codebase (agent.py, subagent.py) and avoids misleading ellipsis on short text.

The walrus operator `_v := simple[_field]` inside `isinstance(...)` makes the validation condition hard to parse at a glance. Extracting the assignment to its own line separates 'get the value' from 'validate it', improving readability without changing behaviour.

In `compact_messages`, both branches of the `if summary_text` block construct a dict with `"role": "user"` duplicated. Extracting the content string into a local variable and building the dict once after the branch eliminates the repetition and makes clear that only the content differs between the two cases.
In `_detect_platform`, the if/elif chain is just a name-mapping from system strings to platform labels. A dict lookup expresses this intent directly and eliminates the branching entirely.

In `_handle_api_routing`, the second `if isinstance(data, list)` is a standalone branch after an early-return, but expressing it as `elif` makes the mutual exclusivity of the two type checks explicit and avoids implying they could both execute.

In the status section of load(), `s.context_percent` is referenced three times in consecutive lines. Extracting it to a local variable eliminates the repetition and makes the bar-color ternary easier to read.

In `safe_write_file`, `os.path.basename(resolved)` is called inline inside an already-complex f-string. Extracting it to a local variable `basename` alongside the existing `parent` makes the temp_path construction easier to read at a glance.

In the format loop of `fetch_link_previews`, sections are built via repeated string concatenation (`+=`). Collecting the lines into a list and joining them expresses the structure more directly and avoids intermediate string allocations.
